intro:
Jeroboam was the name of two kings of the northern kingdom of Israel, the first of whom founded its monarchy and its enduring apostasy.

Jeroboam I, son of Nebat, an Ephraimite, was a capable officer over Solomon’s forced labour. The prophet Ahijah met him and tore a new garment into twelve pieces, giving him ten as a sign that he would rule ten tribes because of Solomon’s idolatry (1 Kings 11:29-31). After Solomon’s death, when Rehoboam threatened the people with heavier burdens, the northern tribes revolted and made Jeroboam king (c. 930 BC) (1 Kings 12:16-20). Fearing that pilgrimages to the temple in Jerusalem would win his people back to the house of David, he set up two golden calves — one at Bethel, one at Dan — saying, “Behold your gods, O Israel, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt” (1 Kings 12:28-30). He installed non-Levitical priests and a rival feast (1 Kings 12:31-33). For this “the sin of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, which he made Israel to sin” became the recurring indictment against which every later northern king is measured (1 Kings 15:34; 2 Kings 17:21-23).

Jeroboam II (c. 793–753 BC), great-grandson of Jehu, presided over the longest and most prosperous reign in the North, restoring its borders from Lebo-hamath to the Sea of the Arabah just as the prophet Jonah had foretold (2 Kings 14:23-27). Yet he too “did evil… he did not depart from all the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat.” His outwardly golden age masked deep social injustice and empty religion, which the prophets Amos and Hosea fearlessly exposed — Amos prophesying judgment against the house of Jeroboam (Amos 7:9-11; Hosea 1:1).

The two Jeroboams together frame the tragedy of the northern kingdom: founded in political rebellion and false worship, sustained even in prosperity by the same idolatry, and finally swept into Assyrian exile for refusing to turn from “the sin of Jeroboam.” His name stands in Scripture as the byword for the leader who, for the sake of holding power, leads a whole people away from the true worship of God.
